does: This paper analyzes the philosophical foundations underlying AI alignment by defending three interconnected propositions: that normative and technical aspects of alignment are interdependent and mutually illuminating, that clarity about alignment goals requires distinguishing between instruction-following, intention-matching, preference satisfaction (both revealed and ideal), interest-respecting, and value-alignment approaches, and that the core theoretical challenge involves identifying fair principles that command reflective endorsement across moral disagreement rather than discovering objectively true moral principles. The paper advocates for a principle-based approach combining these distinct alignment targets systematically, and concludes by outlining three candidate methods for identifying such fair principles in the face of persistent moral pluralism.
connects: This work engages with decades of philosophical research on moral disagreement, reflective equilibrium, and fairness while bringing these frameworks to bear on contemporary AI safety research. It builds on prior technical work in preference learning and specification gaming that highlighted how different alignment specifications (following instructions versus maximizing preferences) can diverge, and on philosophical analyses of the value specification problem in AI. The paper responds to existing literature questioning whether we can identify stable human values for AI systems to pursue, extending debates from normative political philosophy about fair governance under disagreement into the AI alignment domain.
enables: This framework opens pathways for cooperation between technical AI researchers and philosophers by reframing alignment not as a problem of discovering moral truth but as finding workable, defensible principles acceptable across value differences. It provides conceptual scaffolding for designing multi-stakeholder deliberative processes for AI governance and specification. The emphasis on fair principles rather than true values suggests new research directions in social choice theory, democratic theory, and practical reasoning applied to AI, and could inform development of alignment methods that remain legitimate and stable even when no consensus exists on ultimate normative questions.
